ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bun Bo Hue. Closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_03311737) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bun Bo Hue. A0CKW (noodle-based soup) is the closest defensible parent category in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bun Bo Hue or a sufficiently close noodle soup parent that would be clinically meaningful.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Bun Bo Hue as served in a restaurant typically weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, noodles, meat, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Bun Bo Hue (Vietnamese Spicy Beef Noodle Soup) — nutrient profile estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese noodle soups and USDA FoodData Central component data for beef, pork, rice noodles, and lemongrass broth
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables; USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Bo Hue as a complete dish. Values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Vietnamese beef noodle soup analogues with USDA FoodData Central data for individual components (beef shank, pork hock, rice noodles, lemongrass broth). The per-100g figures reflect a typical assembled bowl at approximately 60% broth, 20% noodles, 20% meat by weight. Values are consistent with published ranges for similar Vietnamese noodle soups.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — rice noodles and mixed noodle soup analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bun Bo Hue. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI approximately 53–61, mean ~57) and mixed noodle soup analogues. The dominant glycemic contributor is the round rice noodle (bun). The broth and meat components contribute negligible glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 35.5) / 100 ≈ 19.5, using 500 g serving with 7.1 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from shrimp paste, fish sauce, and seasoned broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Bun Bo Hue to occasional consumption and request reduced fish sauce and shrimp paste, as a standard 500 g bowl may deliver approximately 2100 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index from rice noodles with partial mitigation from protein and fat in broth
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of rice noodles in Bun Bo Hue, as the estimated glycemic load of approximately 19.5 per bowl is moderate; choosing a smaller noodle portion and increasing the meat and herb component can help blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork hock and beef shank combined with high sodium from fermented condiments
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Bun Bo Hue in moderation due to its combined saturated fat load from pork and beef and its high sodium content from shrimp paste and fish sauce, both of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bun Bo Hue (Bún Bò Huế) is a Vietnamese spicy noodle soup originating from the imperial city of Hue in Central Vietnam. It is considered one of Vietnam's most complex and flavourful noodle soups, distinct from the milder pho of Hanoi. The broth is built on a long-simmered base of beef bones and pork hocks, intensely flavoured with lemongrass, shrimp paste (mam ruoc), fish sauce, and dried chilli, giving it a characteristic deep red-orange colour and a bold, spicy, and slightly fermented aroma. The noodles used are thick, round rice vermicelli (bun), which are softer and chewier than the flat rice noodles used in pho. Toppings typically include slices of beef shank, pork hock, and optionally pork blood cake (tiet canh), served with a garnish plate of bean sprouts, banana blossom, fresh herbs, lime, and chilli. Nutritionally, a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 340 kcal, 26 g protein, 12 g fat, and 35.5 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium due to shrimp paste and fish sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (medium) based on the rice noodle component. It is gluten-free and dairy-free but contains shellfish allergens from shrimp paste. In Singapore, Bun Bo Hue is served in Vietnamese restaurants and is popular as a hearty, warming meal. It is not halal due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Bun Bo Hue as a complete dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues and USDA FoodData Central component data. GI is estimated from rice noodle analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the estimation methodology is systematic and the component data sources are approved primary sources.
notes: Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae), B vitamins (vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg), folate (folate_mcg_dfe), vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg), vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg), phosphorus (phosphorus_mg), magnesium (magnesium_mg), zinc (zinc_mg), and selenium (selenium_mcg) are set to -1 as these micronutrients are not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA analogues used for estimation and cannot be reliably derived from component-level USDA data for this composite dish without introducing unacceptable uncertainty. Trans fat is 0.0 as beef and pork in this dish are not industrially hydrogenated and naturally occurring trans fat in ruminant meat is negligible at the per-100g level for this broth-dominant dish. The dish is not halal due to pork hock and optional pork blood cake. Shrimp paste (mam ruoc) is a shellfish allergen. The cuisine field is set to Vietnamese reflecting the dish's origin; the input record listed Singapore as cuisine, which reflects the serving context (field survey data) rather than culinary origin. Region of origin is Hue, Central Vietnam.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Hue Spicy Beef and Pork Noodle Soup' is precise and non-promotional. Vietnamese name 'Bún Bò Huế' present. Singaporean multilingual requirements satisfied: Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi all present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros present and numeric: energy_kcal 68.0, protein_g 5.2, fat_g 2.4, carbohydrate_g 7.1. Source is FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central, both approved primary sources. Estimation methodology documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 0.3, sodium_mg 420.0, saturated_fat_g 0.9, sugar_g 0.5 — all present as numeric values and plausible for a broth-dominant noodle soup.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 55 (numeric, >0), gi_category 'medium', glycemic_load_per_serving 19.5, gi_source documented with University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 peer-reviewed journal. Analogous estimate basis clearly stated.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent FOODON_03311737 (noodle soup food product) is defensible. FoodEx2 A0CKW (soup, noodle-based) is defensible closest_parent. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes invented. Match types use only allowed values: exact, closest_parent, none.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1350 characters). Covers what the food is, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, GI/GL), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat + sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name fields correctly set.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases list contains 15 entries covering English variants, Vietnamese forms, romanized forms, Chinese script (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. aliases_by_language is fully consistent with flat aliases list — every string in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Initial draft produced but rejected due to invalid JSON output (empty response from gemini).
Iteration 2: Second draft produced but again rejected due to invalid JSON (empty response from gemini).

Iteration 3: Full valid JSON produced from scratch. Cuisine corrected from 'Singapore' (serving context) to 'Vietnamese' (culinary origin). Multilingual names added for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi (Singaporean serving context requires these), and Vietnamese (dish origin). All -1 sentinel values documented in notes. Ontology match_type values restricted to allowed set. aliases_by_language verified consistent with flat aliases. GI set to numeric 55 with analogous estimate basis documented. Trans fat 0.0 justified in notes.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: PASS — all six hard gates pass and all eight Q criteria score 1/1. The record is a well-documented estimated composite for Bun Bo Hue, a Vietnamese spicy noodle soup served in Singapore. Nutrient values derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database rice noodle analogues. Multilingual coverage satisfies both Singaporean (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) and Vietnamese (Vietnamese script) requirements. Ontology uses honest closest_parent matches with gap notes. Health context provides specific clinical guidance for hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Score: 8/8.
